   *(Important distinction)*: For council, installed costs and power purchase agreement (PPA) rates matter more than module prices. Even with current softening in solar prices, utility-scale solar PPA pricing in Oklahoma averages $30–40/MWh, while OG&E retail/residential rates are $100–140/MWh. That's a 60–70% spread.
   
   *(Caveats)*: Supply chain, interconnection, and labor keep total project costs from falling as fast as modules. But the long-term trend is undeniable: solar and batteries are now among the cheapest forms of new capacity in most U.S. markets, including the South.
   
   *(Council framing)*: "We’re not betting on experimental tech. Utility-scale solar now costs less than 75 cents per watt to install, and batteries have dropped over 80% since 2010. The national average solar PPA is $35/MWh while Oklahoma utilities charge $100–140/MWh to retail customers. content.text:
You’re very close, but there’s a critical distinction that will actually **strengthen your credibility** when colleagues ask for specifics: the ~90% figure applies to **solar modules** and **battery packs**, not **installed system costs**. Here’s the precise breakdown with current 2024–2025 data, plus how to phrase it so it’s bulletproof on the council floor.

---

### 🔋 **Cost Declines: Module/Pack vs. Installed Project**

| Component | 2010 Cost | 2024/2025 Cost | Decline | What Matters for City Procurement |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|---------|-----------------------------------|
| **Solar Modules** (panels only) | ~$2.80–$3.00/W | ~$0.10–$0.15/W | **~90%** | Sets ceiling for hardware, but not total project cost |
| **Installed Utility-Scale Solar** (panels + racking + inverters + interconnection + labor) | ~$3.50–$4.00/W | ~$0.75–$0.90/W | **~70–75%** | What you actually pay for a city facility system |
| **Lithium-Ion Battery Packs** | ~$1,000+/kWh | ~$100–$140/kWh | **~85–90%** | Hardware cost; doesn’t include BMS, controls, site work |
| **Installed Storage Systems** | ~$1,200+/kWh | ~$350–$450/kWh | **~60–65%** | Total delivered cost for city-grade microgrid/buffer storage |

**Sources:** NREL 2024 Annual Technology Baseline, BloombergNEF Battery Price Survey (2023/2024), IEA PVPS, DOE SunShot Initiative historical benchmarks.

---

### 💡 **What This Means for Municipal Procurement**
- **Solar PPA pricing** in the Southwest averages **$30–45/MWh** today. Oklahoma utilities charge retail/commercial customers **$100–140/MWh**. That’s a **60–70% spread** even before accounting for demand charge reduction or inflation hedging.
- **Battery storage** now makes economic sense not just for energy arbitrage, but for **peak shaving and backup**. A 2–4 hour system at a water plant or fire station typically pays for itself in 4–6 years through demand charge avoidance and avoided diesel/emergency generator costs.
- **Soft costs** (engineering, permitting, interconnection, labor) have stabilized but now represent ~30–40% of total project cost. They won’t drive prices down further, but they also haven’t erased the long-term trend.

---

### 🗣️ **Council-Ready Phrasing (Accurate & Defensible)**
> *“The hardware alone has dropped 70–90% since 2010, and installed systems are now at historic lows. National data shows utility-scale solar costs about 80 cents per watt to install, with power purchase agreements averaging $35/MWh. Meanwhile, our utility bills at municipal facilities are effectively paying $100–140/MWh when you factor in demand charges and rate escalations. We’re not buying experimental technology. We’re locking in a 25-year fixed rate that’s already 60% below what the legacy grid charges us today.”*

---

### ⚠️ **Anticipate These Pushback Points (and Responses)**
| Pushback | Data-Backed Response |
|----------|----------------------|
| *“Prices have stabilized or ticked up since 2022.”* | True for modules/batteries, but system costs remain ~75% below 2010 levels. More importantly, PPA pricing and LCOE still undercut retail rates by a wide margin in OK. |
| *“Soft costs and labor keep municipal projects expensive.”* | Standardized specs, design-build contracts, and program management can cut soft costs by 20–30%. Even at full installed cost, the math still favors solar + storage over legacy rates. |
| *“What if battery degradation or inverter failures eat the savings?”* | Modern systems come with 10–20 year performance guarantees. RFPs require minimum 80% capacity retention at Year 10, and O&M is contractually fixed. |

---

### 📊 **Recommended Reference Table for Your Brief**
If you want to hand colleagues a one-liner they can fact-check in 10 seconds:
> **Solar installed cost trend:** $3.80/W (2010) → $0.82/W (2024) = **~78% drop**  
> **Battery system cost trend:** $1,250/kWh (2015) → $410/kWh (2024) = **~67% drop**  
> **Solar PPA vs. OG&E retail rate:** ~$38/MWh vs. ~$115/MWh = **~67% savings locked in**

*(All figures from NREL, BNEF, EIA, and recent SOO/OG&E tariff filings.)*

---

### ✅ **Next Step**
If you’d like, I can:
- Drop this into a clean, council-handout format with logos/headers
- Build a simple 2-cell comparison slide (2010 vs. 2025 installed costs + PPA spread)
- Draft a 3-sentence verbal talking point you can use in committee to anchor the cost argument

Which would save you the most time before the hearing?
